name: Feijoada (Black Bean Stew)
steps: Cook bacon and sausage 5 minutes.
Add beans and 1/2 cup water, simmer 20 minutes.
steps_detailed: Cook the bacon in a heavy pot over medium heat until it renders its fat, 4-5 minutes.
Add the sausage and onion, cooking 5-6 minutes until the onion softens.
Add the garlic and bay leaf, cooking 1 minute.
Add the beans and broth. Simmer 20-25 minutes, mashing some of the beans against the side of the pot to thicken the stew - feijoada should be hearty and thick, not soupy.
Remove the bay leaf, top with parsley, and serve - Brazil's national dish, traditionally simmered for hours with a variety of pork cuts.

name: Moqueca (Brazilian Fish Stew)
steps: Simmer bell pepper in coconut milk 5 minutes.
Add fish, simmer 10-12 minutes until cooked through.
steps_detailed: Rub the fish with lime juice and a pinch of salt.

Heat the palm oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Cook the onion, bell pepper, and garlic 6-7 minutes until softened - palm oil's distinctive color and flavor are essential to authentic moqueca, especially the Bahian style.
Add the tomatoes and coconut milk, simmer 8 minutes.
Nestle the fish into the sauce, cover, and simmer 10-12 minutes until it flakes easily.
Top with cilantro before serving.

name: Brazilian Grilled Chicken (Frango Grelhado)
steps: Marinate chicken in lime juice and garlic 15 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear 5-6 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Whisk the lime juice, garlic, oil, and black pepper together. Coat the chicken and marinate at least 20 minutes.
Grill or pan-sear over medium-high heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until cooked through (165F).
Rest 5 minutes, top with parsley, and serve - simple, bright flavors are the everyday backbone of Brazilian home cooking.

name: Picadinho (Brazilian Beef Hash)
steps: Brown the beef, 5-6 minutes.
Add tomatoes, simmer 12 minutes.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Brown the beef, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion, garlic, and bell pepper, cooking 5-6 minutes until softened.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 12-15 minutes until thickened.
Cook the rice per package directions and serve the beef hash over it, topped with parsley - a comforting everyday Brazilian staple.

name: Brazilian Shrimp Stew
steps: Simmer tomatoes and coconut milk 8 minutes.
Add shrimp, simmer 5-6 minutes until pink and cooked through.
steps_detailed: Heat the palm oil in a pan over medium heat. Cook the onion and garlic 4-5 minutes.
Add the tomatoes and coconut milk, simmer 8-10 minutes until slightly thickened.
Add the shrimp and simmer 5-6 minutes until pink and just cooked through.
Finish with lime juice and top with cilantro - a simpler cousin of moqueca, quick enough for a weeknight.

name: Churrasco-Style Steak
steps: Rub steak with garlic and oil, season generously with salt.
Grill 4-5 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Rub the steak generously with garlic, oil, coarse salt, and black pepper - churrasco relies on quality meat and simple seasoning rather than a heavy marinade.
Grill over high heat, 4-5 minutes per side for medium-rare, adjusting for your preferred doneness.
Rest 5 minutes before slicing against the grain.
Top with parsley and serve - the centerpiece of any Brazilian churrascaria.

name: Frango com Quiabo (Chicken with Okra)
steps: Brown the chicken, 5-6 minutes.
Add okra and tomatoes, simmer 15 minutes.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium-high heat and brown the chicken, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion and garlic, cooking 4-5 minutes until softened.

Add the tomatoes and okra, simmer 15-18 minutes until the chicken is cooked through - the okra will naturally thicken the sauce as it cooks, a texture that's central to this dish rather than something to avoid.
Finish with lime juice before serving.

name: Brazilian Rice and Beans Bowl
steps: Warm the beans with onion.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pan over medium heat. Cook the onion and garlic 4-5 minutes.
Add the beans and bay leaf, simmering 10-12 minutes until heated through, mashing a few beans to thicken slightly.
Remove the bay leaf. Cook the rice per package directions and serve the beans over it, topped with parsley - rice and beans (arroz e feijão) is the daily foundation of Brazilian meals.

name: Coxinha-Style Chicken Bites
steps: Mix mashed potato with shredded chicken, shape into small teardrops.
Coat in breadcrumbs, bake at 400F for 20 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Cook the onion and garlic in a splash of oil until soft, then mix with the shredded chicken.
Mash the warm potato until smooth and season with a pinch of salt.
Shape the potato around a small spoonful of chicken filling, forming the classic teardrop shape coxinha is known for.
Dip each piece in beaten egg, then coat in panko.
Bake at 400F for 20-22 minutes until golden and crisp, or shallow-fry 2-3 minutes per side for the traditional deep-fried result.
Serve warm - one of Brazil's most beloved street foods and party snacks.

name: Vinagrete Salad with Chicken
steps: Toss tomato and bell pepper with vinegar and oil.
Serve alongside the chicken.
steps_detailed: Whisk the vinegar and oil together.
Toss the tomato, bell pepper, and onion with the dressing - vinagrete is a bright, chunky relish traditionally served alongside grilled meats.
Fold in the parsley.
Serve alongside the sliced chicken.

name: Brazilian Fish Soup
steps: Simmer tomatoes and coconut milk 8 minutes.
Add fish, simmer 10 minutes until cooked through.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium heat. Cook the onion and garlic 4-5 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, broth, and coconut milk. Simmer 8-10 minutes.
Add the fish, cover, and simmer 10-12 minutes until it flakes easily.
Top with cilantro before serving.

name: Pão de Queijo Sandwich
steps: Mix tapioca flour, cheese, and milk into a sticky dough, form small rolls.
Bake at 400F for 20 minutes until puffed and golden.
Split and fill with ham.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 400F.
Warm the milk and oil together until just simmering, then pour over the tapioca flour and mix - this partially cooks the starch, which is key to pão de queijo's chewy texture.
Once cooled slightly, mix in the egg and Parmesan until a sticky, stretchy dough forms.
Shape into small rolls and place on a lined baking sheet.
Bake 18-20 minutes until puffed and lightly golden, with a chewy, cheesy interior.
Split while warm and fill with ham for a satisfying lunch.

name: Pão de Queijo (Brazilian Cheese Bread)
steps: Mix tapioca flour, cheese, and warmed milk into a sticky dough.
Shape into small rolls, bake at 400F for 18-20 minutes.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 400F.
Warm the milk and oil together until just simmering, then pour over the tapioca flour and mix well.
Once cooled slightly, mix in the egg and Parmesan until a sticky, stretchy dough forms.
Shape into small rolls and bake 18-20 minutes until puffed and golden - a beloved breakfast staple across Brazil, best eaten warm.

name: Brazilian Fruit and Yogurt Bowl
steps: Top yogurt with banana and honey.
steps_detailed: Spoon the yogurt into a bowl.
Top with sliced banana, chopped cashews, and shredded coconut, reflecting Brazil's abundant tropical fruit and nut tradition.
Drizzle with honey before serving.

name: Coxinha Bites (Snack Size)
steps: Mix potato and chicken, shape into small bites, coat in breadcrumbs.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes.
steps_detailed: Mix the mashed potato with the shredded chicken and garlic.
Shape into small bite-sized pieces.
Dip in beaten egg, then coat in panko.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes until golden - a lighter, snack-sized version of the classic teardrop coxinha.

name: Spiced Roasted Cashews (Brazilian)
steps: Toss cashews with lime juice, toast in a dry skillet 3-4 minutes.
steps_detailed: Toss the cashews with lime juice and a pinch of cayenne.
Toast in a dry skillet over medium-low heat, 4-5 minutes, stirring often, until fragrant.
Cool completely before eating - Brazil is one of the world's largest cashew producers, and roasted, salted cashews are a common snack nationwide.

name: Banana Chips (Brazilian)
steps: Toss banana slices with oil, bake at 300F for 25-30 minutes until crisp.

ingredients_full.note: sliced thin, slightly underripe works best

steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 300F. Slice the banana as thin and evenly as possible.
Toss with oil and cinnamon.
Spread in a single layer on a lined baking sheet and bake 25-30 minutes, flipping halfway, until dried and crisp.
Cool completely - they crisp further as they cool.

name: Brigadeiro Bites
steps: Cook condensed milk, cocoa, and butter over low heat, stirring, 8-10 minutes until thick.
Cool, roll into small balls.
steps_detailed: Combine the condensed milk, cocoa powder, and butter in a saucepan over low heat.
Cook, stirring constantly, 8-10 minutes until the mixture thickens enough to pull away from the sides of the pan when stirred.
Stir in the vanilla, then let cool completely - it needs to be fully cool to roll properly.
Roll into small balls and coat in the reserved cocoa powder.
Brazil's most iconic sweet, traditionally served at every birthday party.

name: Beijinho (Coconut Sweets)
steps: Cook condensed milk, coconut, and butter over low heat, stirring, 8-10 minutes until thick.
Cool, roll into small balls, coat in extra coconut.
steps_detailed: Combine the condensed milk, coconut, and butter in a saucepan over low heat.
Cook, stirring constantly, 8-10 minutes until thickened enough to pull away from the sides of the pan.
Let cool completely, then roll into small balls.
Coat in the reserved shredded coconut - beijinho ('little kiss') is brigadeiro's coconut sibling, equally beloved at Brazilian celebrations.

name: Bacalhau-Style Baked Cod
steps: Layer potato, onion, and cod in a baking dish, drizzle with oil.
Bake at 400F for 25-30 minutes until the potato is tender and fish flakes easily.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 400F.
Layer half the potato and onion in a baking dish, drizzle with half the oil, and season lightly.
Nestle the cod on top, scatter the garlic and olives around it, then layer the remaining potato and onion on top.
Drizzle with the remaining oil.
Bake 25-30 minutes until the potato is tender and the fish flakes easily.
Top with parsley before serving - inspired by Portugal's bacalhau tradition, adapted with Brazil's own coastal seafood.
